title: 10 best herbal teas for dry scalp

remedy_properties: Antioxidant activity: Sesame tea's antioxidant properties help protect the scalp from damage caused by free radicals, which can contribute to dryness and irritation.
Anti-inflammatory effects: The anti-inflammatory compounds present in sesame tea, such as sesamin and sesamolin, can help reduce redness, itching, and flakiness associated with a dry scalp.
Moisturizing properties: Sesame tea contains emollients like sesamine that can help lock in moisture, soothe dryness, and soften the scalp, reducing flaking and itchiness.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Sesamum indicum, also known as sesame, teas helps with dry scalp because of its rich antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. The tea is extracted from the seeds of the sesame plant, which are known to have moisturizing and soothing effects on the scalp. The antioxidants present in sesame tea help to nourish and protect the scalp, reducing dryness and itchiness. Additionally, sesame tea's anti-inflammatory properties help to calm irritation and promote a healthy scalp environment, leading to a relief from dry scalp conditions.
remedy_constituents: Fatty acids: Fatty acids, particularly oleic acid, help with dry scalp by moisturizing and nourishing the skin, reducing inflammation and promoting a healthy scalp environment.
Phenolic compounds (specifically, sesamol): Phenolic compounds, such as sesamol, have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help soothe and calm the scalp, reducing dryness and itchiness.

Sesamin: Sesamin, a lignan present in Sesamum indicum, has been shown to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help reduce inflammation and promote a healthy scalp, alleviating dry scalp conditions.
remedy_parts: Seeds: They are used due to their antifungal and antibacterial properties that help soothe and calm the scalp.
Leaves: They are used because they contain anti-inflammatory compounds that help reduce redness and irritation associated with dry scalp.
Flowers: They are used for their cooling and soothing effects, which can help calm dry, itchy scalps.
remedy_recipe: Measure 2 teaspoons of dried sesamum indicum flowers for every 8 ounces of water required.
Heat 1 cup of water in a saucepan over medium heat to boiling point for 5 minutes.
Add the measured sesamum indicum flowers to the boiling water and let steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a separate container.
Discard the solids and serve the tea hot or allow it to cool for later consumption.

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Stinging nettle tea contains anti-inflammatory compounds like salicylic acid, which helps reduce inflammation and itching associated with dry scalp, promoting healthier skin.
Antioxidants: The antioxidant properties in stinging nettle tea help combat oxidative stress and damage caused by free radicals, which can contribute to dry scalp conditions and promote healing.

Purifying and detoxifying properties: Stinging nettle tea has been traditionally used to purify the blood and remove toxins, which may help eliminate underlying causes of dry scalp such as impurities in the bloodstream or skin.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Urtica dioica, also known as stinging nettle, teas helps with dry scalp because of its rich antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. The tea has been found to stimulate blood flow and reduce irritation on the scalp, creating a healthy environment for hair growth. Urtica dioica's antifungal and antibacterial properties also help to control scalp infections, which can contribute to dryness and flakiness. By soothing and calming the scalp, Urtica dioica tea promotes a balanced scalp ecosystem, leading to a healthier and more hydrated scalp.
remedy_constituents: Alkaloids: Alkaloids present in Urtica dioica, such as aucubin and isochlorogenic acid, help reduce inflammation and soothe dry scalp irritation.
Flavonoids: Flavonoids like quercetin and kaempferol in Urtica dioica have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help calm dry scalp redness and itching.

Saponins: Saponins found in Urtica dioica, like urticine and 3,4-dioxygenated isomers, exhibit anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties, contributing to a healthy scalp environment and reducing dryness.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Urtica dioica leaves are commonly used to make teas for dry scalp because they contain compounds like histamine and chlorogenic acid, which have anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties.
Stems: Urtica dioica stems are also used to make teas for dry scalp as they contain antioxidants and other beneficial compounds that can soothe and calm the scalp.
Roots: Urtica dioica roots are another part used to make teas for dry scalp, as they contain inulin, a prebiotic that can promote healthy scalp conditions and reduce inflammation.
remedy_recipe: Harvest a handful of fresh urtica dioica leaves and stems in the early morning when they are at their highest potency level.
Rinse the urtica dioica leaves and stems with cold filtered water to remove any dirt or debris.
Steep 1 teaspoon of dried urtica dioica leaves in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5 to 7 minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine mesh sieve into a cup to remove any solids.
Let the tea cool to a comfortable temperature before drinking it within 30 minutes of preparation.

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: Aloe vera's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as aloin and aloe-emodin, help reduce redness and swelling associated with dry scalp conditions, promoting a soothing and calming effect on the skin.

Antifungal and antibacterial properties: The antimicrobial compounds in aloe tea, including salicylic acid and glucomannan, help combat fungal and bacterial infections that can contribute to dry scalp issues, preventing further irritation and inflammation.

Moisturizing and soothing properties: Aloe vera's natural moisturizers, such as vitamin E and fatty acids, help lock in moisture and soothe dry, irritated skin on the scalp, reducing flaking and itchiness.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Aloe vera, also known as aloe, teas helps with dry scalp because of its soothing and nourishing properties. The gel extracted from the aloe plant is rich in vitamins A, C, and E, as well as minerals like calcium and potassium. These nutrients help to moisturize and calm the scalp, reducing inflammation and itching associated with dryness. The anti-inflammatory compounds in aloe vera also promote healthy hair growth and improve scalp circulation, leading to a healthier and more balanced scalp.
remedy_constituents: Aloe-emodin: This anthraquinone compound has anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties, which help soothe and calm the scalp, reducing irritation and flakiness associated with dry scalp.
Saponins: These glycosides have emollient and moisturizing properties, which help lock in moisture and reduce dryness, promoting a healthy scalp environment.

Alkaloids (specifically, aloin and aloe-emetin): These compounds have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which help reduce redness, itching, and irritation caused by dry scalp, while also preventing infections.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The gel inside Aloe vera leaves is commonly used due to its soothing and hydrating properties, which help to calm and moisturize the scalp.
Leaves: The juice extracted from Aloe vera leaves is rich in vitamins and minerals, providing essential nutrients to promote healthy scalp growth and alleviate dryness.
Leaves: The outer layer of Aloe vera leaves contains salicylic acid, which helps to exfoliate and remove dead skin cells from the scalp, reducing flakiness and itchiness.
remedy_recipe: Gather 200ml of aloe vera gel from 2-3 aloe vera leaves that have been allowed to mature for 3-4 months.
Combine the aloe vera gel with 1 liter of boiling water and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a large container to remove excess pulp and gel.
Add a sweetener such as honey to taste and stir well to combine with the herbal tea.
Serve the aloe vera tea warm or chilled and enjoy as a soothing digestive aid.

remedy_properties: Inflammation reduction: Dandelion tea's anti-inflammatory properties can soothe and calm an irritated scalp, reducing redness and itching associated with dryness.

Astringent properties: Dandelion tea's astringent nature can help balance the scalp's natural pH, reducing oiliness and irritation that can contribute to dryness and flakiness.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Taraxacum officinale, also known as dandelion, teas helps with dry scalp because it is rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds. remedy_constituents: Apigenin: This flavonoid helps alleviate dry scalp by exhibiting anti-inflammatory properties, reducing redness and itching associated with scalp conditions.

Taraxasterol: A triterpenoid saponin, taraxasterol has been shown to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which help soothe and protect the scalp from irritation and infection, addressing dry scalp conditions.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of Taraxacum officinale are often used to make teas due to their high content of saponins, which have anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties that can help soothe and calm the scalp.
Flowers: Taraxacum officinale flowers are commonly used to make teas because they contain flavonoids and phenolic acids that can help reduce inflammation and promote the growth of new hair.

Roots: The roots of Taraxacum officinale are sometimes used to make teas because they contain sesquiterpenes and other compounds that can help to reduce inflammation and promote a healthy scalp environment.
remedy_recipe: Harvest taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers in the early morning for optimal freshness and potency.
Rinse the harvested taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers with cold water to remove dirt and debris.
Combine 1-2 teaspoons of dried taraxacum officinale with 8 ounces of boiling water for a standard serving.
Steep the taraxacum officinale mixture for 5-7 minutes to allow the active compounds to infuse.
Strain the taraxacum officinale tea and serve hot or chilled immediately after preparation.

remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: Kansas coneflower tea's antimicrobial properties can help combat fungal and bacterial infections on the scalp, reducing inflammation and irritation associated with dry scalp conditions.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds in Kansas coneflower tea can soothe and calm irritated skin, reducing redness and swelling caused by dry scalp conditions like dandruff and psoriasis.
Antioxidant properties: Kansas coneflower tea's antioxidants help protect the scalp from damage caused by free radicals, which can contribute to dry scalp conditions, and promote healthy hair growth.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Echinacea angustifolia, also known as Kansas coneflower, teas helps with dry scalp because of its anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties. Additionally, Echinacea angustifolia's moisturizing properties can help lock in moisture, soothing and calming the dry scalp. remedy_recipe: Gather 30-60 grams of dried echinacea angustifolia root and 1 teaspoon of dried mint leaves for added flavor.
Boil 1 liter of water in a pot and bring to a rolling boil for 5 minutes.
Add the dried echinacea angustifolia root to the boiling water and steep for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a separate container.
Discard the solids and let the tea cool to a comfortable temperature for consumption within 10 minutes.

Measure out 1 teaspoon of the dried rosmarinus officinalis leaves per 8 ounces of boiling water for the infusion.

remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of Cymbopogon citratus are commonly used to make teas for dry scalp due to their high content of citral, a compound that helps to soothe and calm the scalp.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 20 fresh stalks of the plant by cutting them close to the ground using scissors or pruning shears.
Rinse the harvested stalks under cold running water for 2 minutes to remove impurities.
Chop the stalks into smaller pieces using a sharp knife or a herb cutter for 5 minutes.
Steep 1 teaspoon of the chopped stalks in boiling water for 5-7 minutes to release the oils.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve and serve hot or chilled immediately.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 20-30 grams of fresh or 10-20 grams of dried mentha x piperita leaves and flowers in the morning.
Clean the harvested plant material thoroughly by gently rinsing it with cold water for 5 minutes.
Steep 1 teaspoon of dried plant material or 2 teaspoons of fresh plant material in 250ml of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup to remove the plant material.
Allow the tea to cool to a comfortable temperature before serving and consuming within 30 minutes.

Steep 1 teaspoon of dried thymus vulgaris leaves or 2 teaspoons of fresh leaves in 8 ounces of boiling water.

Heat water to a simmer and pour it over the calendula petals let steep for 5 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup discard the solids and add honey if desired adjust sweetness.
Allow the tea to cool slightly then apply it directly to the scalp massage in circular motions.

remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried glycyrrhiza glabra root powder into a heat-resistant cup.
Boil one cup of pure water in a kettle or on the stovetop and let it cool for five minutes.
Combine the cooled water and powdered root, stirring gently to prevent lumps from forming.
Let the mixture steep for 10-15 minutes to allow the active compounds to infuse into the liquid.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried verbena officinalis leaves and flowers for the tea preparation.
Measure one teaspoon of dried verbena officinalis or two teaspoons of fresh verbena officinalis for each cup of boiling water.
Combine the measured verbena officinalis with 200ml of boiling water in a heat-resistant teapot or cup.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes to allow the herbal properties to infuse into the water.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Fennel teas helps with dry scalp because of its antifungal and anti-inflammatory properties, which work together to soothe and calm irritated skin. Seeds: Fennel seeds are rich in antioxidants and flavonoids, which help reduce inflammation and promote healthy skin and hair growth, addressing the root cause of dry scalp.
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried fennel seeds into a small muslin bag or tea infuser for every eight ounces water.
Bring the water to a boil in a saucepan and pour it over the fennel seeds in the muslin bag or tea infuser.
Allow the mixture to steep for 10-15 minutes or until the liquid has reduced slightly and the flavors have melded together.
Strain the tea into a cup by removing the muslin bag or tea infuser from the saucepan and discarding the solids.
Drink one to two cups of the tea per day for up to four weeks as needed.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Field horsetail teas helps with dry scalp because of its rich content of antioxidants, vitamins, and minerals. The tea's high silicon content has been shown to improve skin elasticity and reduce inflammation, which can help soothe an itchy and flaky scalp. Additionally, the anti-inflammatory properties of field horsetail may help calm redness and irritation caused by dry scalp conditions like dandruff and psoriasis. Antifungal and antibacterial properties: The tea's antifungal and antibacterial properties help to combat fungal and bacterial infections that can cause dry, flaky scalp conditions like seborrheic dermatitis.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried equisetum arvense leaves and stems in the morning.
Combine one teaspoon of dried equisetum arvense with one cup boiling water for 10 minutes steeping.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a clean glass container.
Store the cooled tea in an airtight container and refrigerate for up to five days use.
Drink the tea twice daily, morning and evening, for two weeks to help alleviate dry scalp.

supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to dry scalp that are treated with herbal teas are skin issues such as eczema, acne, and psoriasis. Herbal teas like peppermint, chamomile, and calendula have anti-inflammatory and soothing properties that can help calm irritated skin and reduce redness. These teas can be used topically as a compress or added to bath water for relief from dry, itchy skin.
intro_desc: Herbal teas for dry scalp are natural remedies that help alleviate dryness and irritation on the scalp by promoting blood circulation, reducing inflammation, and soothing itchiness. The best herbal teas for dry scalp include Melaleuca alternifolia, Rosmarinus officinalis, Cymbopogon citratus, Aloe barbadensis, Urtica dioica, Thymus vulgaris, Saponaria officinalis, Lavandula angustifolia, Eucalyptus globulus, and Santalum album. If left untreated, dry scalp can lead to hair loss, embarrassment, and decreased self-confidence, causing significant distress in daily life, affecting social interactions and overall well-being.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best combination of herbal teas that help with dry scalp is a blend of peppermint, chamomile, and rosemary. Peppermint tea helps to reduce inflammation and soothe irritation, while chamomile tea calms and cools the scalp, reducing redness and itchiness. Rosemary tea promotes blood circulation, which helps to stimulate hair growth and nourish the scalp. Drinking a combination of these teas can provide relief from dry scalp symptoms and promote a healthy scalp environment. This blend can be consumed hot or cold, sweetened with honey or lemon to taste.
intro_study: For example, according to a study published by Current topics in medicinal chemistry, dandruff is a scalp disorder that predominantly affects the male populace, and its treatment using topical agents and synthetic drugs often results in specific side effects such as dryness or oiliness of the scalp. The study found that essential oils and extracts from plants could be valuable in the treatment and prevention of dandruff, and alternative therapies such as tea tree oil, thyme, and Aloe vera have been reported to demonstrate anti-dandruff activity by disrupting the microbial growth associated with dandruff formation. As a result, herbal teas, which are made from these same plants, can also be beneficial for a dry scalp due to their antimicrobial properties that help to control the growth of pathogens responsible for dandruff.

remedies.remedy_desc: Melaleuca alternifolia teas helps with dry scalp because of its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The tea, derived from the leaves of the Melaleuca alternifolia plant, contains compounds like cineole and terpinen-4-ol that help soothe and calm the scalp. These properties aid in reducing inflammation and redness associated with dry scalp conditions, promoting a healthy scalp environment. Regular consumption of Melaleuca alternifolia tea may also help to balance the scalp's natural oil production and promote hair growth.
remedy_constituents: Cineole: This compound helps with dry scalp by reducing inflammation and promoting the growth of healthy skin cells.
Limonene: It aids in dry scalp by exhibiting antimicrobial properties that help eliminate fungal infections, which can contribute to scalp dryness.
Terpinen-4-ol: This terpene helps with dry scalp by reducing bacterial growth and promoting wound healing in the scalp, thus alleviating dryness and itchiness.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Leaves are a primary source of essential oils in Melaleuca alternifolia, which are used to soothe and treat dry scalp conditions.
Buds: Buds contain a high concentration of essential oils, including tea tree oil, which is known for its antifungal and antibacterial properties that help to combat dry scalp infections.
Leaves: In some cases, leaves are used in combination with other parts, or in their pure form, to create teas that promote scalp health and reduce dryness.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh leaves of melaleuca alternifolia in the early morning after the dew has evaporated.
Dry the leaves in a single layer at 105°f for 2 hours to preserve their potency.
Steep 1 tablespoon of dried leaves in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the liquid through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup to remove the solids.
Drink the tea immediately after straining to experience its full benefits within 10-15 minutes.

remedies.remedy_desc: Rosmarinus officinalis teas helps with dry scalp because it has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that promote a healthy scalp environment. The essential oils present in rosemary tea, such as camphor and bornyl acetate, stimulate blood circulation to the scalp, which can help reduce dryness and itchiness. Additionally, rosemary's antifungal properties combat scalp infections that can contribute to dry scalp conditions. Regular consumption of rosemary tea may also improve overall scalp health and reduce the appearance of flakes.
remedy_constituents: Rosmarinic Acid: This phenolic compound helps alleviate dry scalp by reducing inflammation and preventing the growth of fungal and bacterial organisms that can contribute to scalp irritation.
Carnosic Acid: This phenolic compound helps to soothe and calm the scalp, reducing inflammation and promoting healthy skin cell growth, which can aid in addressing dry scalp conditions.
Bornyl Acetate: This terpene helps to reduce inflammation and promote blood flow to the scalp, which can aid in reducing dryness and promoting a healthy scalp environment.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Rich in antioxidants and essential oils, which help soothe and calm the scalp.
Flowers: High in rosmarinic acid, a compound that has anti-inflammatory properties and can help reduce dandruff and itchiness.
Leaves: Also contains camphor, a compound that helps to reduce inflammation and promote a healthy scalp.
remedy_recipe: Gather 1 tablespoon of dried rosmarinus officinalis leaves and 1 cup of boiling water for the tea.
Steep the rosmarinus officinalis leaves in the boiling water for 5 to 7 minutes.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup to remove the leaves.
Add honey or sugar to taste to sweeten the herbal tea if desired.
Serve the warm tea immediately and enjoy after preparing for 10 minutes.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cymbopogon citratus teas helps with dry scalp because of its antifungal and antibacterial properties. The essential oils found in this tea, such as geraniol and limonene, have been shown to reduce inflammation and promote a healthy scalp environment. remedy_recipe: Harvest 20-30 fresh cymbopogon citratus leaves for optimal flavor and aroma.
Rinse the harvested leaves in cold running water to remove dirt and debris.
Steep 2-3 teaspoons of dried cymbopogon citratus leaves in 8 ounces of boiling water.
Allow the mixture to infuse for 5-7 minutes to release the herbal properties.
Strain the tea into a cup and serve immediately while still warm.

remedies.remedy_desc: Aloe barbadensis teas helps with dry scalp because of its soothing and moisturizing properties. Barks: The barks of Aloe barbadensis contain anti-inflammatory compounds that can help to reduce inflammation and irritation on the scalp, promoting a healthy scalp environment.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 3-4 fresh aloe barbadensis leaves with green skin from mature plants and clean them thoroughly.
Chop the leaves into small pieces and blend them with 1 cup of water in a blender.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a large bowl for 10 minutes.
Add honey or sugar to taste to the strained liquid and stir well for 2 minutes.
Refrigerate the herbal tea in airtight containers for up to 3 days and serve chilled.

remedies.remedy_desc: Urtica dioica teas helps with dry scalp because of its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The plant's leaves contain urticoside, which soothes and calms irritated skin, reducing inflammation and itchiness associated with dry scalp. Triterpenoids: Exhibits anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, helping to eliminate fungal infections and reduce inflammation that may contribute to dry scalp conditions.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 2-3 ounces of fresh or 4-6 ounces of dried urtica dioica leaves and stems in late spring.
Rinse the harvested leaves and stems with cold water to remove dirt and debris.
Steep 1 teaspoon of dried urtica dioica in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve to remove solids.
Store the prepared tea in a glass container in the refrigerator for up to 3 days.

remedies.remedy_desc: Thymus vulgaris teas helps with dry scalp because of its antifungal and antibacterial properties, which combat scalp infections that can lead to dryness and irritation. The tea's anti-inflammatory properties also soothe and calm the scalp, reducing redness and itchiness. remedy_parts: Leaves: Used due to their antifungal properties, which help combat scalp infections and promote healthy scalp conditions.

remedy_recipe: Harvest the thymus vulgaris leaves by pinching or cutting the stems at the node when they are in full bloom.
Dry the harvested leaves in a single layer on paper towels for 1 to 3 weeks in a cool dry place.
Measure out one teaspoon of dried thymus vulgaris leaves per 8 ounces of boiling water to make a strong brew.
Steep the leaves in the boiling water for 5 to 10 minutes to allow the flavors and oils to infuse.
Strain the liquid through a fine-mesh sieve and serve the herbal tea hot or cold within a few hours.

remedies.remedy_desc: Saponaria officinalis teas helps with dry scalp because of its soothing and moisturizing properties. remedy_parts: Leaves: Used due to their antifungal and anti-inflammatory properties, which help soothe and calm an itchy scalp.

remedy_recipe: Harvest saponaria officinalis flowers and leaves when they are in full bloom and the plant is in optimal condition.
Dry the harvested plant material in a warm dark place for 1 week to 2 weeks.
Measure out 1 teaspoon of dried saponaria officinalis flowers and leaves per 8 ounces of water for brewing.
Steep the dried plant material in boiling water for 5 to 10 minutes to release its active compounds.
Strain the tea and let it cool to room temperature before serving.

remedies.remedy_desc: Lavandula angustifolia teas helps with dry scalp because of its soothing and calming properties, which promote relaxation and reduce stress. The tea's antifungal and antibacterial agents help to control scalp infections, while its antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds calm and nourish the scalp, reducing irritation and flaking. This results in a reduction in dryness and itchiness.
remedy_constituents: Linalool: This terpene has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help soothe and calm the scalp, reducing irritation and dryness.

Rosmarinic acid: This phenolic compound has antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help protect the scalp from oxidative stress and inflammation, promoting a balanced scalp ecosystem.
remedy_parts: Leaves: They are the most commonly used part, as they are rich in essential oils and provide a soothing effect on the scalp.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 20-30 fresh lavandula angustifolia flowers in the early morning for maximum oil content retention.
Clean the harvested flowers gently with lukewarm water to remove dirt and debris immediately.
Dry the cleaned flowers in a single layer using a food dehydrator at 95°f for 2 hours.
Store the dried flowers in airtight containers to preserve their fragrance and flavor for up to 6 months.
Steep 1 teaspoon of dried lavandula angustifolia flowers in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.

remedies.remedy_desc: Eucalyptus globulus teas helps with dry scalp because of its anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties. The tea's active compounds, such as cineole, help to reduce inflammation and soothe the scalp, promoting a healthy environment for hair growth. remedy_constituents: Cineole: Helps to reduce inflammation and promote a healthy scalp environment, alleviating dryness and itchiness associated with dry scalp.

Caffeic acid: Acts as an antioxidant and anti-inflammatory agent, helping to soothe and calm the scalp, reduce irritation, and promote a healthy scalp environment.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Used due to their high concentration of eucalyptol, a natural decongestant that helps relieve dry scalp symptoms.
Buds: Employed for their antifungal and antibacterial properties, which aid in treating scalp infections that may exacerbate dryness.
Barks: Utilized for their anti-inflammatory properties, which help soothe and calm the scalp, reducing irritation and promoting healthy skin.
remedy_recipe: Harvest the eucalyptus globulus leaves in the early morning after the dew has evaporated for optimal potency.
Dry the freshly harvested leaves in a warm oven at 150°f for 1 hour to remove excess moisture.
Measure out 1 teaspoon of dried eucalyptus globulus leaves per 8 oz of boiling water for a standard serving.
Steep the dried leaves in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes to allow the flavors and oils to infuse.
Strain the tea into a cup and discard the solids before serving hot or iced as desired.

remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of Santalum album are used to make teas for dry scalp due to their antifungal and antibacterial properties.
Barks: The barks of Santalum album are used to make teas for dry scalp due to their anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties that help soothe and calm the scalp.

remedy_recipe: Gather 3-6 grams of dried santalum album and place it in a glass container for storage.
Measure 1-2 teaspoons of dried santalum album per 8 ounces of water for the perfect brewing ratio.
Heat 8 ounces of water in a saucepan to a temperature of 212 degrees fahrenheit.
Steep the dried santalum album in the heated water for 5-7 minutes to release its fragrance.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup to remove the herbs completely.
